flashcards.front: What is the core principle of divide-and-conquer strategy?
flashcards.back: Divide problem into subproblems, solve recursively, then combine solutions

flashcards.front: What makes greedy algorithms different from dynamic programming?
flashcards.back: Greedy makes locally optimal choices without reconsidering, while DP uses overlapping subproblems

flashcards.front: Name two problems solved using backtracking
What is memoization in dynamic programming?
flashcards.back: Technique of storing computed results to avoid redundant calculations

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of merge sort?
flashcards.back: O(n log n) due to divide-and-conquer approach

flashcards.front: When is branch-and-bound preferred over backtracking?
flashcards.back: When we need optimal solution with pruning of unnecessary branches

flashcards.front: What is the key characteristic of randomized algorithms?
flashcards.back: Use random numbers to make decisions, producing different outputs for same input
Shortest path problem - optimal path contains optimal subpaths

flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of dynamic programming over recursion?
flashcards.back: Avoids recomputation of overlapping subproblems through memoization

flashcards.front: Name two applications of greedy algorithms
flashcards.back: Huffman coding, Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm

flashcards.front: What is the role of selection function in greedy algorithms?
flashcards.back: Determines which candidate to add to solution set based on heuristic

flashcards.front: What is backtracking's space complexity for N-Queens?
flashcards.back: O(n) for recursion stack, compared to O(n!) brute force solution
